summary: Policy-backed control for Smart Multi-Homed Name Resolution. The local Microsoft DnsClient.admx file explicitly documents the same registry key, value name, and enabled/disabled values that the app uses.

setting.name: Smart Multi-Homed Name Resolution
setting.casual_explanation: When a PC has multiple network paths, Windows can try name lookups more aggressively across them. This policy decides whether that smart multi-homed behavior stays on.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want more predictable network resolution, less cross-interface guessing, or a stricter network posture.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling the feature can make name resolution more conservative and predictable, but it may also remove some fallback behavior on systems with multiple network interfaces.
setting.professional_notes: The policy writes DisableSmartNameResolution under the DNSClient policy key. In DnsClient.admx, enabled policy state writes 1 and disabled state writes 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force the feature and normal Windows behavior applies.

allowed_values.label: Feature enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and smart multi-homed name resolution remains on.

allowed_values.label: Feature disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is enabled and smart multi-homed name resolution is turned off.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableSmartNameResolution = 1, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default

states.rationale: Policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: Explicit value 0 and missing are not the same registry state, but they both correspond to the feature being enabled.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that do not need a forced DNS client hardening stance
avoid_for: Strictly managed or hardened network configurations

states.rationale: Leave the policy unset and preserve Windows baseline behavior.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want default Windows DNS client behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to disable cross-interface smart resolution behavior on purpose.
tradeoffs: Windows retains its standard multi-homed resolution behavior.

recommended_profiles.label: Predictable multi-interface networking
intended_for: Users who want stricter DNS resolution behavior
avoid_for: People who depend on aggressive fallback behavior across interfaces
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a more conservative and explicit DNS client behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: The environment expects the feature to help resolve names across multiple interfaces.
tradeoffs: Some fallback convenience can be reduced.

evidence.title: Microsoft ADMX_DnsClient Policy CSP
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the smart multi-homed name resolution policy and its registry mapping.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft DnsClient.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 for DisableSmartNameResolution.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableSmartNameResolution = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft DnsClient.admx file explicitly defines DNS_SmartMultiHomedNameResolution at Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient with valueName DisableSmartNameResolution and enabledValue 1 / disabledValue 0, which matches the app's registry surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="DNS_SmartMultiHomedNameResolution" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient" valueName="DisableSmartNameResolution"> <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same registry key and value name that the app writes and confirms the 1/0 mapping.
